name: Vitamin B12
dose: 50-100 mcg daily or 1,000-2,000 mcg twice weekly for maintenance
whenToTake: Any time of day, with or without food; morning or early afternoon if high doses feel stimulating.
toxicity: No established upper limit and low toxicity risk, though rare acne-like reactions are reported.
testingNotes: Choose clear dosage labels and plant-based capsules or chewables. Blood work may include serum B12, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine.
notes: The highest-priority supplement for people eating fully plant-based because unfortified plant foods are not reliable B12 sources.

name: Vitamin D3
dose: Common maintenance range is 1,000-2,000 IU daily, adjusted to blood levels and clinician advice.
whenToTake: Take with a meal that contains fat to improve absorption.
toxicity: Toxicity is rare but possible with chronic very high doses; excess can raise blood calcium dangerously.
testingNotes: Use 25-hydroxyvitamin D blood tests to personalize dosing, especially with limited sun exposure or known deficiency.
notes: Most useful when sun exposure is low, blood levels are low, or a clinician recommends maintenance supplementation.

name: Algal Omega-3 DHA/EPA
dose: 250-500 mg combined DHA/EPA daily for general support.
whenToTake: Take with a fat-containing meal; refrigerating capsules may reduce algae burps.
toxicity: Generally safe at usual doses; high intakes may increase bleeding risk, especially with blood thinners or surgery.
testingNotes: Prefer products with oxidation and contaminant testing. Read the back label for actual DHA plus EPA, not just total oil.
notes: A direct plant-based source of long-chain omega-3s that bypasses the inefficient ALA-to-DHA conversion pathway.

name: Iron
form: Ferrous bisglycinate, ferrous sulfate, ferrous fumarate, or clinician-recommended forms
dose: Use only when labs or a clinician indicate a need; dose depends on ferritin, hemoglobin, symptoms, and tolerance.
whenToTake: Often taken away from calcium, tea, and coffee; vitamin C may improve absorption. Follow clinician guidance if prescribed.
toxicity: Iron can accumulate and cause harm. Overdose is dangerous, especially for children, and some people should avoid supplemental iron.
testingNotes: Review ferritin, hemoglobin, transferrin saturation, serum iron, and TIBC before supplementing. Do not rely on fatigue alone.
notes: Useful when iron deficiency or low stores are confirmed; not a just-in-case supplement for plant-based diets.

name: Zinc
form: Zinc citrate, picolinate, gluconate, or bisglycinate
dose: Use targeted doses when intake is low or labs and symptoms support it; avoid chronic high-dose use unless supervised.
whenToTake: Take with a small meal if it causes nausea. Keep high-dose zinc separate from iron or copper plans unless guided.
toxicity: Long-term high-dose zinc can cause nausea and copper deficiency, which may lead to anemia or neurological symptoms.
testingNotes: Serum zinc can help but is imperfect. Interpret with diet pattern, symptoms, inflammation, and clinician advice.
notes: Most useful when dietary zinc is consistently low, symptoms fit, or testing and clinical context support supplementation.

name: Creatine Monohydrate
dose: 3-5 g daily; loading is optional and not required for most people.
whenToTake: Any time daily; consistency matters more than timing. Taking with a meal can reduce stomach upset.
toxicity: Well studied at standard doses for healthy adults, but people with kidney disease should consult a clinician first.
testingNotes: Look for single-ingredient powders and third-party sport certification if competing in tested sports.
notes: Optional but useful for strength training, sprint performance, and lean-mass goals. Most creatine is synthetic and not animal-derived.
